behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: The failure is purely request-construction logic: the module forgets or incorrectly formats taxonomy labels. A Unit test mocking the API client can verify the constructed request includes all labels by asserting on call arguments. No real API call is needed; the boundary is entirely within the classification module.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API client. Call classify() with a test document. Assert that the mocked API was called exactly once with request arguments that include the complete list of configured taxonomy labels.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test with mocked API and call-argument assertions directly verifies the behavior without real I/O.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns 'unknown' instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: The failure is validation logic executing locally: the module forwards an invalid label instead of mapping it to 'unknown'. A Unit test mocking the API to return an invalid label and asserting the returned value is 'unknown' catches this failure. No real API behavior is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return a label outside the configured taxonomy (e.g., 'invalid_category'). Call classify(). Assert that the returned result is 'unknown', not the unexpected label.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test mocking the API and asserting return values directly verifies the behavior.

behaviors.description: The classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls.
behaviors.justification: To catch the real failure (results differ on repeated calls despite same input), you must call the real API. A Unit test with a mocked API trivially passes (mock always returns same result) and would not catch missing caching or inconsistencies. The behavior depends on whether the module implements deterministic caching against an AI API's potential non-determinism. Only an Agentic test calling the real API (or realistic simulation) can verify this holds in practice.
behaviors.test_description: Call classify(document) twice with the same input. Verify both results are identical. This test must go through the actual API integration (potentially with seeding/deterministic endpoint) rather than a trivial mock. If the module implements caching, both calls should be served consistently despite API non-determinism.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Agentic test with real API calls verifies true consistency. A Unit test mocking the API and checking that it's called only once would verify caching implementation, but doesn't test real-world behavior against actual API non-determinism.

behaviors.description: The number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: The failure is that actual tokens consumed exceed the budget. This depends entirely on real API behavior—what tokens the API actually uses vary by model and implementation. A mock returning fixed token counts will not catch real over-budget scenarios. To verify the module correctly handles token accounting and enforces budget against a real AI API's actual response, you must call the real API and measure actual token usage.
behaviors.test_description: Call classify() with a typical document and extract token usage from the API response. Assert tokens_used <= configured_budget. Repeat with edge cases (long documents, special characters) to ensure budget is never exceeded in realistic scenarios against the real API.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Testing token consumption inherently requires real API calls (Agentic) to measure what is actually used. Mocked token counts would not catch real over-budget failures.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: The core failure is the module returning an error to the caller when it should retry instead. This is error-handling logic that can be tested at Unit level: mock the API to return a rate-limit error (HTTP 429) on the first call and success on the second, then verify the module succeeds instead of propagating the error. However, timing correctness (whether the module actually waits the configured duration) requires System/Integration testing.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API client such that the first call raises/returns a rate-limit error (429 or equivalent) and the second call succeeds with a valid result. Call classify(). Assert that the returned result is the valid classification (not an error), proving the module retried instead of failing. Optionally, mock time and verify the module waited the configured backoff duration.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test catches the core retry behavior (did it retry?). System/Integration tests would verify wait timing. The minimum to catch 'error propagation instead of retry' is Unit.
